topic: A person finds out their biography was written without their knowledge
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias traced the embossed lettering on the first page of the archival document. The prose detailed his life—the triumphs, the setbacks, the supposed turning points—all meticulously crafted. He felt a slight, almost imperceptible tightening in his chest, a sensation he usually dismissed as indigestion. He flipped to a section describing his early career, a passage that sounded remarkably like something he’d overheard in a crowded coffee shop years ago. He didn't feel anger, nor did he feel surprise. It was just a quiet acknowledgment of an inconvenient truth, like noticing a smudge on a windowpane. He closed the file, placing it back on the shelf with a soft, deliberate click. He stood up, stretched his arms above his head, and walked toward the window, watching the city lights blur. Nothing shifted inside him.

[story 2]

The email arrived at midday, stark white against the dark background of his screen. It contained a full, annotated biography. Reading it felt like observing a distant, poorly rehearsed play. There was a strange detachment as he absorbed the facts—dates, quotes, motivations—that were undeniably his, yet felt alien, like reading someone else’s diary. He leaned back in his chair, the worn leather creaking beneath him. His fingers drummed a slow, rhythmic pattern on the desk surface. He didn't bother to reply. The weight of the revelation settled over him, heavy but strangely unmoving, like a stone dropped into still water. He simply minimized the window and started scrolling through unrelated news feeds.

[story 3]

When the curator handed him the bound volume, Clara simply took it, her expression neutral. "This is the official narrative," she stated, her voice flat. Elias took the book, the paper cool beneath his fingertips. He skimmed the text, his eyes moving across the carefully constructed narrative. A flicker of something—a momentary pause in his breathing—crossed his face, but it vanished as quickly as it appeared. He didn't argue or demand an explanation. Instead, he turned the book over, examining the spine, the binding, treating it as an object of study rather than a personal affront. He placed it gently back on the table, the silence in the room amplifying the sheer lack of reaction he was exhibiting.
